claim: The AI supply chain is the most immature part of the industry—downstream inefficiencies exist between cloud providers, downstream developers, startups, and end users; these haven't been adequately addressed
actionable: When building AI products, anticipate supply chain constraints; collaborate with cloud providers to streamline data access and model fine-tuning workflows.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Latency-optimized hardware (like Groq's LPU) is necessary for real-time AI agents in production—legacy GPU infrastructure cannot handle thousands of agents with tool calls, web searches, and MCP connections simultaneously
actionable: For production AI agents with tool calling and real-time requirements, evaluate specialized AI hardware (LPUs) not just GPUs; measure token throughput and latency, not just inference time.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Building evaluation systems as a foundation is critical—use LLMs as judges across multiple dimensions (visual design, output quality, workflow coherence) rather than relying on standard benchmarks
actionable: Develop custom evaluation frameworks using LLMs as multi-dimensional judges; evaluate on dimensions relevant to your product, not just standard benchmarks.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Reinforcement learning fine-tuning can make open-source models competitive with proprietary models on specific tasks—fine-tuned open models learn to use agent tools reliably without knowledge injection
actionable: Use reinforcement learning fine-tuning on open models to specialize them for tool use and agent workflows; build internal evals to validate improvement.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Don't default to expensive frontier lab models when open-source models work for tool calling and general tasks—consider model diversity and multi-model workflows for cost optimization
actionable: Benchmark your specific tool-calling scenarios with open-source models; use smaller models for simple tasks and frontier models only when necessary.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Each new frontier model release shouldn't trigger immediate migration—use vibe testing to evaluate whether the new model improves your specific use cases before switching infrastructure
actionable: When new models release, test them against your internal benchmarks before committing infrastructure changes; monitor tool use quality and task completion rates.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Defining fairness and responsible AI principles in generative AI is harder than in traditional ML—LLMs produce free-form text requiring human annotation for stereotyping behavior rather than formal definitions
actionable: For generative AI RAI, use human annotation to identify stereotyping and biased outputs; implement guardrails trained on these annotations rather than relying on formal fairness definitions.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Regulatory frameworks often lack technical specificity—GDPR and others mention fairness and privacy without defining measurement or enforcement; regulations focus on process rather than technical requirements
actionable: When evaluating regulatory requirements, look for technical specifications; if guidance is vague, implement best practices (differential privacy, human oversight) rather than waiting for clarity.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: The pendulum swung too far toward general-purpose models—when you know your specific use case, build targeted predictive models rather than specializing general powerful models for that task
actionable: For well-defined problems, consider building targeted models or fine-tuned specialists rather than always using general-purpose frontier models.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: The biggest bottleneck in AI growth is the lack of community and collaboration between AI experts and domain experts—pairing AI specialists with healthcare, economics, or other domain experts yields the highest impact
actionable: Actively seek collaborations between AI engineers and domain experts in healthcare, research, policy; build products that require both AI and domain expertise.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: Building useful AI systems in practice requires much more than a few lines of code—understanding full-stack options (on-device, fine-tuned, open-source, frontier models) enables appropriate solution matching
actionable: Learn the full AI stack (on-device ML, fine-tuning, open-source, cloud models) to match solutions to problems; don't assume one model tier fits all use cases.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth

claim: The most exciting frontier in next 6-12 months may be LLMs accelerating LLM research itself—if frontier models can help labs develop better models faster, it could create a tipping point in capability growth
actionable: Monitor research on using AI to accelerate AI research; this could dramatically compress model development cycles if realized.
source_title: AI Dev 25 x NYC | Panel discussion: Breaking the Limits of AI Growth
